---
subject: "What I’ve been reading"
from: "Marginal REVOLUTION <donotreply@wordpress.com>"
to: ""
date: 2025-09-15 05:14:03
labels: ["CATEGORY_PERSONAL", "INBOX", "Tyler Cowen", "UNREAD"]
label_ids: ["CATEGORY_PERSONAL", "INBOX", "Label_2466199532210586699", "UNREAD"]
---
1. Mark Gilbert, [Italy Reborn: From Fascism to Democracy](http://marginalrevolution.com?action=user_content_redirect&uuid=ce0a778c4f198943f90ff7b6c2df91ba5130ce23b891a3c85846adc26d51ed6f&blog_id=42693868&post_id=91404&user_id=262258391&subs_id=225460721&signature=969d43464e1a1ba76e8c76a0c1eefaa5&email_name=new-post&user_email=ishan.mail@gmail.com&encoded_&email_id=8e152d89f89984b2e832c0d861a640f3). How Italy built a democracy after WWII, more or less out of nothing. An optimistic and good book. De Gasperi was a great man, and essential to the building out of a democratic Italy, yet today his name is hardly known.
2. Dave Edmonds, [Death in a Shallow Pond: A Philosopher, A Drowning Child, and Strangers in Need](http://marginalrevolution.com?action=user_content_redirect&uuid=3c74896731379982ee19c58f2c8c260a49fb51d575815300655597cf4f598c2e&blog_id=42693868&post_id=91404&user_id=262258391&subs_id=225460721&signature=f920331b91f8ed69bdf6799b80c3dd82&email_name=new-post&user_email=ishan.mail@gmail.com&encoded_&email_id=8e152d89f89984b2e832c0d861a640f3). An engaging but also intellectually serious history of some strands of utilitarianism and effective altruism. I was happy to blurb this book, you may recall Edmonds also wrote the excellent biography of Derek Parfit.
3. Arundhati Roy, [Mother Mary Comes to Me](http://marginalrevolution.com?action=user_content_redirect&uuid=0f7ddfd4514fb9cc97a80b902928a57ff5793047479914c297c8b779929568fb&blog_id=42693868&post_id=91404&user_id=262258391&subs_id=225460721&signature=eb14921d442375bf4ba88d0923d13750&email_name=new-post&user_email=ishan.mail@gmail.com&encoded_&email_id=8e152d89f89984b2e832c0d861a640f3). Well-written, but at some point I started wondering why I should care. OK, Mama was a pain in the ass, but then what? You had to take care of her when she was old. I guess I prefer whaling tales?
4. Chuck Klosterman, [Football](http://marginalrevolution.com?action=user_content_redirect&uuid=1fa125bf64c849e44aed2e39c44d0eef090f79db3836079d92aee00f4e2b4ef8&blog_id=42693868&post_id=91404&user_id=262258391&subs_id=225460721&signature=72f7d2eb7d88d94b80df9e588976a3f1&email_name=new-post&user_email=ishan.mail@gmail.com&encoded_&email_id=8e152d89f89984b2e832c0d861a640f3). An excellent and highly conceptual book about America's favorite sport. Could this be the best book on (American) football ever?
5. Jordana Pomeroy, [Daring: The Life and Art of Elisabeth Vigée Le Brun](http://marginalrevolution.com?action=user_content_redirect&uuid=53b634773a4dbfa4532eb93bb4696384e853c4de9907e00a6c28f54dd136b516&blog_id=42693868&post_id=91404&user_id=262258391&subs_id=225460721&signature=19ee7e7d2808559214743b57443eae8d&email_name=new-post&user_email=ishan.mail@gmail.com&encoded_&email_id=8e152d89f89984b2e832c0d861a640f3). Both a book and a picture book rolled into one. This new release is a very good introduction to her life, her art, and her role as semi-official court painter for Marie de Antoinette. She remains an underrated artist.
6. Michael Lentz, [Schattenfroh](http://marginalrevolution.com?action=user_content_redirect&uuid=a79fea56bf1c1e661ed3353e045271bc0f3ef8f9a9e5cf2764bdce295b7768d3&blog_id=42693868&post_id=91404&user_id=262258391&subs_id=225460721&signature=753837ddeee4dbcfc08bc795bc395092&email_name=new-post&user_email=ishan.mail@gmail.com&encoded_&email_id=8e152d89f89984b2e832c0d861a640f3). About one thousand pages, it is receiving buzz as a new novel to master, some are calling it "the new Solenoid" (not a positive for everyone, I do understand). I have tried parts in English and parts in German, but still I do not get it. Does it have a plot? Any humor? For purposes of norming, I am a big fan of James Joyce's Ulysses. I will try it again, however. At least the author was not complacent.
